findings: The hot-pink section headline reads as incomplete in the screenshot: “Make the scroll count in” lands without the payoff visible, which makes the section feel broken rather than bold.
The primary CTA in the hero is visually too small and too lightweight compared with the oversized headline, so the page sells attitude harder than action.
The tiny cyan plus and pink chevron in the hero feel stray and disconnected from the layout, reading like leftover decoration instead of intentional brand assets.
The long supporting copy on black uses a dim gray that drops contrast and gets hard to read at this size, especially in the hero and proof sections.
The three black cards in the white section are visually identical, so the eye gets no clear entry point or priority across the row.

The page relies on large black-white-pink horizontal bands with very little playful TikTok-specific visual character, which makes the system feel more generic campaign landing page than creator-native.
Several headlines are so wide and heavy that line breaks feel awkward and crowded, especially the hero and the “stories.” section, weakening hierarchy instead of sharpening it.

events.prompt: Audit the landing page against our brand rules, focusing on adherence to our color palette, font usage, and overall tone. Ensure the page is free of business jargon and maintains a playful, direct sty

events.doing: auditing the page against brand rules

findings: The hero headline uses two emphasis colors at once: a cyan plus mark and pink word/chevron, which breaks the one-accent emphasis rule and makes the headline feel less disciplined.

In the pink 'How it works' section, the emphasized final word in the headline is effectively invisible against the same pink background, so the headline reads as incomplete ('Make less fuss. Get more').
The large dot pattern on the white section is rendered in light grey, introducing a non-brand neutral that reads off-brand and filler-like.
The hero supporting copy runs too wide in a single long line, weakening scanability and making the hierarchy feel loose under such a bold headline.
The oversized grey dot block takes up too much visual weight relative to the actual message in the 'The problem' section, so the decorative element competes with the content instead of supporting it.

The three black cards in the white section feel dense and text-heavy, with small body copy sitting in large dark boxes, which hurts legibility and makes the section feel more like placeholder content than polished brand storytelling.
The hero leaves a large empty black void on the right, making the opening feel unbalanced and unfinished instead of bold and intentional.
The small cyan plus and pink chevron in the hero read like stray marks rather than purposeful brand elements because they float with too little visual weight.
The hot-pink 'How it works' headline appears cut off as shown, reading only 'Make the scroll count in' and feeling incomplete.
The 'Proof' section uses vague stat cards like '60s', '1 feed', and 'More taps' that look like placeholder claims instead of concrete evidence.
The page relies on the same black rounded card block three sections in a row, which flattens hierarchy and makes the layout feel repetitive.
Body copy inside the black cards is noticeably small and dense relative to the oversized headlines, which weakens scanning and legibility.
The pink emphasis treatment is over-relied on across section headlines, so the highlighted words stop feeling special and the hierarchy loses punch.
The CTA row in the hero feels visually undersized compared with the headline block, so the primary action does not carry enough weight.
The hero headline mixes cyan and pink emphasis at once: the cyan plus mark competes with the pink word and pink chevron, breaking the one-accent focus.
The large pale grey dot pattern in the white section introduces a neutral visual field that feels off-palette instead of using only black, white, and approved brand colors.
The dot pattern on the right is visibly cropped off at the edge, which makes the section look unfinished and template-like.
The problem headline breaks awkwardly so the emphasized pink word 'TikToks.' sits as an isolated fragment in the middle of the sentence, weakening readability and hierarchy.
There is a large block of empty black space under the hero CTAs before the next section starts, making the opening composition feel sparse and underdesigned.
The repeated black cards in both the white section and pink section are visually monotonous, so the page starts to feel rigid instead of playful or creatively paced.

The hero subhead and supporting paragraph are too similar in size and weight, so the copy hierarchy under the main headline feels mushy.
The body copy inside the black cards is small and dense relative to the oversized headings, which hurts fast scan legibility.
The hot-pink 'How it works' band loses a clear focal point because the headline has no visible emphasis word, so that section reads flatter than the others.

The section chips ('Why it works', 'How it works', 'Proof') are visually too small and timid compared with the headline scale, so section starts are easy to miss.

events.reasoning: The page has strong contrast, a clear hero hierarchy, and mostly sharp, persuasive copy, but the later sections feel less resolved: the white section is airy to the point of sparse, and the pink three-step section looks awkwardly cropped and imbalanced with the oversized black shape colliding with the cards. It reads broadly TikTok-like through the black/white/#FE2C55 palette and direct tone, but brand fit is incomplete because the typography appears generic rather than distinctly Sofia Pro, the cyan accent is essentially absent, and the decorative pale outline/extra shape language weakens the tightly controlled brand system. quality 78, brandAdherence 62 → score 72 (0.6·q + 0.4·b).

findings: The hero headline mixes cyan and pink emphasis marks with pink emphasized text in the same line, so the headline is carrying multiple accent colors instead of a single emphasis color.
The word “Magic!” in the hero reads as hypey promo language, and the oversized pink treatment plus exclamation pushes the tone into forced excitement rather than confident directness.
The large pale section behind “Your ad looks like an ad. That is the problem.” reads as a neutral/light-grey field rather than a black, white, or approved brand-color block.
The hero has a very large empty black gap below the CTA row before the next section starts, which weakens hierarchy and makes the top of the page feel unfinished.
The long problem-section headline stretches too wide across the page, making it feel heavy and harder to scan quickly.

The section labels (“TikTok for Business,” “The problem,” “How it works,” “Proof”) are so small relative to the surrounding type that they read like incidental pills instead of useful hierarchy markers.
The proof cards use oversized headings like “1 clear idea” and “More action” that dominate their cards and create uneven, awkward rhythm across the row.
The page relies almost entirely on flat black, white, and hot pink blocks with very little playful brand character beyond the hero marks, so the overall tone feels severe rather than creatively fun.

events.doing: auditing the page against brand rules

findings: The hero headline is visibly cut off after “Don’t make ads. Make”, so the core message does not fully render.
The second section headline is clipped on the right edge, so “TikToks get watched.” is not fully legible.
The hero has a large expanse of empty pink space with almost no supporting visual device, which makes the top of the page feel unfinished rather than bold and playful.
The cyan brand accent is almost absent in the composition, leaving the page visually dominated by pink/black/white and underplaying TikTok’s signature energy.
The section labels “The Problem,” “How It Works,” and “Proof” read like generic template placeholders instead of distinctive TikTok brand language.
The card layout is overly uniform and repetitive across sections, making the page feel generic SaaS rather than creative, playful, or TikTok-native.
The supporting paragraph text under the black-section headline is too small and visually faint against the oversized heading, weakening hierarchy and scanability.
The hero headline uses two emphasis colors at once: cyan on the plus mark and pink on both “Magic!” and the chevron, which makes the lockup feel noisy instead of disciplined.
The word “Magic!” in the main headline reads hypey and gimmicky, which pushes the tone toward ad-speak instead of confident, direct performance marketing.
The hero has a large block of empty black space on the right, making the top section feel unfinished and weakening the page’s opening visual impact.
The supporting line under the hero is too long in one wide measure, which reduces scanability and weakens the hierarchy under the oversized headline.
The white section headline “Your ad looks like an ad. That is the problem.” is so long and heavy that it becomes a dense wall of text rather than a sharp, punchy message.
The cards in the white section are visually generic black rectangles with minimal brand character, so the layout feels more editorial-template than TikTok-native.
The pink “How it works” section is overwhelmingly saturated across a very large area, which competes with the content and makes the section feel blunt rather than playful.
The step cards in the pink section use tiny outlined number pills that are easy to miss, so the three-step structure does not read strongly at a glance.
The black “Proof” section relies on claim-style statement cards instead of visible proof elements, making the area feel like placeholder messaging rather than evidence.
The oversized card headline “1 clear idea” in the proof row creates awkward internal hierarchy and makes the three proof cards feel inconsistent in visual weight.
Section spacing creates several heavy horizontal bands of black that feel like dead zones between messages, which slows the page rhythm.
The overall page leans very hard on black, white, and pink blocks with little playful variation, so the brand expression feels severe rather than creative.
The hero headline reads as visually incomplete in the screenshot: “Don’t make ads. Make” lands without the payoff, so the core message feels broken.
There is an oversized empty gap between the hero headline and the supporting copy/CTAs, which weakens urgency and makes the top section feel under-designed.
The “Polished ads get skipped. TikToks get watched.” headline is set too large for the container and feels cramped against the right edge, hurting scanability.
The “Make the video. Match the vibe. Drive the click.” headline is also oversized for the width and reads awkwardly at the edge instead of feeling clean and controlled.
The step numbers “01 / 02 / 03” are tiny and low-contrast on the black cards, so the sequence is hard to read at a glance.
The typography looks generic rather than distinctly TikTok-branded; the page reads like a default web sans system instead of a strong brand type voice.
After the hero, the page drops into mostly black-and-white blocks with very little accent color, which strips out TikTok’s playful energy.
The repeated three-up card grids use nearly identical box shapes and sizing in back-to-back sections, flattening hierarchy and making the page feel templated.

findings: The hero headline mixes two accent colors at once: the cyan plus mark and the pink highlighted word/chevron compete inside the same headline.
The second section sits on a light neutral field instead of a black, white, or single brand-color block, which makes the page feel off-brand immediately after the hero.
The hero leaves a large block of empty black space on the right, making the top of the page feel unfinished and weakening the first-screen hierarchy.
The bright pink full-bleed section is visually aggressive but the cards inside it are plain black boxes, so the section feels louder than it feels intentional.
Section chips like “The problem,” “How it works,” and “Proof” are visually tiny relative to the oversized headlines, so they read like leftover UI labels instead of purposeful brand devices.
The card grid repeats the same black rounded rectangle treatment in every section, which makes the page feel templated and visually monotonous instead of playful and creative.
The proof section presents large statement cards but no visible metrics, logos, charts, or evidence cues, so the “Proof” label reads like placeholder framing rather than real proof.
The headline phrasing “Unlock TikTok Magic!” reads hype-heavy and entertainment-first, which pushes tone away from credible business results on the first screen.
The visual rhythm is inconsistent: the first two sections have very different density and whitespace, creating abrupt jumps instead of a controlled brand flow.

The bottom black section uses a cyan dot pattern behind the content area, but it reads like decorative filler rather than a deliberate brand asset because it has no clear compositional role in the screenshot.

There is no visible headline, subhead, CTA, or any marketing copy, so the page fails to communicate a results-oriented value proposition to marketers.
The illustration is too small relative to the full canvas, which weakens visual hierarchy and reduces impact for a brand that should feel direct and confident.
The composition reads like a floating stock-style icon cluster rather than a branded campaign visual, which makes the page feel generic instead of distinctly TikTok for Business.
Large areas of unused neutral space make the layout feel more like a placeholder than a finished marketing page.

findings: The hero headline uses two emphasis colors at once: the aqua plus mark and the pink emphasized word/chevron compete inside the same headline.
The overall page relies on repetitive black text cards with almost no visual variety or playful brand devices, so it reads rigid and generic instead of distinctly TikTok.
The 'Proof' section has a large area of empty black space around the headline, making the layout feel unfinished and weakening scan flow.
The 'Proof' headline is so oversized that it breaks into multiple short lines, which hurts hierarchy and makes the section feel heavy and awkward.
The page is overwhelmingly copy-first and card-first, with no visible creator-style imagery or lively visual storytelling, which makes the tone feel more like a generic B2B landing page than TikTok.
The section labels and cards repeat the same pill-plus-rectangle pattern so consistently that the page loses momentum and starts to feel templated.
The hero leaves a large empty black void under the CTAs, so the above-the-fold area feels unfinished and wastes the strongest conversion real estate.
The hero headline uses two different emphasis colors at once (cyan symbol and pink word/chevron), which breaks the one-accent reading and makes the emphasis feel noisy.
The second section headline over-emphasizes only the word “TikToks.” in pink, making the sentence feel typographically gimmicky instead of clean and direct.
The hot-pink “How it works” headline is split into two distant chunks, with “Get the customer.” pushed far right, which weakens reading flow and hierarchy.
The small white sentence under the pink section headline has weak legibility against the saturated background at that size.
The step numbers 01, 02, 03 are tiny and low-contrast inside the black cards, so the sequence is hard to scan quickly.
The page relies almost entirely on flat text blocks and cards with no visible product, creator, or proof imagery, so it reads more like a placeholder layout than a finished TikTok campaign page.
The overall look is too austere and static for TikTok: long black/white slabs and plain cards dominate, with very little visual playfulness beyond a few accent marks.
The secondary hero CTA is visually too quiet on the black background, so the action hierarchy collapses into one obvious button and one nearly recessed option.

events.reasoning: The page has strong top-level hierarchy, high contrast, and real, persuasive copy; the hero headline and CTA block read immediately, and the feature cards are legible and consistent. Quality drops because the hero leaves a large dead black area to the right, and the hot-pink 'How it works' section feels crowded and a bit clumsy, especially the center white card with cramped copy and weaker balance. Brand fit is mostly strong through the black/white/#FE2C55 palette, bold direct tone, flat surfaces, and no visible gradients or shadows, but it does not feel fully unmistakable because the cyan accent is absent and the layout reads more like a generic campaign landing page than a distinctly TikTok-native system. quality 76, brandAdherence 84 → score 79 (0.6·q + 0.4·b).

findings: The hero CTAs are visually underweighted versus the oversized headline, so the primary action does not command enough attention at first glance.
The right column in the white "The problem" section is mostly empty space with one short paragraph, making the layout feel unfinished and weakening scan hierarchy.
The three black cards in the white section all have nearly identical weight, color, and structure, which makes the content feel repetitive instead of playful or distinctly branded.
The step numbers "01", "02", and "03" are too faint against the black cards and are easy to miss, reducing legibility of the sequence.
The cyan accent in the hero subhead reads like a tiny stray symbol rather than a clear brand device because it is too small and visually detached.
Several body-copy lines run very wide across the canvas, especially in the hero and white section, which hurts readability on a page built around fast scanning.
The page relies heavily on plain black, white, and pink blocks with very little visible playful brand expression, making the overall tone feel more stark than creative.
The secondary outline CTA in the hero is easy to overlook on the black background because its visual contrast and fill are much weaker than the primary button.

It reads somewhat TikTok through the black and razzmatazz hero, but brand adherence drops because the design visibly leans on neutral white/light-gray surfaces, barely uses the cyan accent, and the overall typography/layout feel more generic SaaS than unmistakably playful TikTok-for-Business. quality 74, brandAdherence 44 → score 62 (0.6·q + 0.4·b, capped at 69 for brand miss).

events.reasoning: The page has strong top-level hierarchy, high contrast, and sharp non-placeholder copy, especially in the hero and first white section, but the design becomes repetitive with samey card blocks, oversized empty spans, and a weaker rhythm as the page continues into the pink section. It broadly fits TikTok for Business through the black/white/#FE2C55 palette and direct tone, but it reads more austere editorial than playful TikTok, and the brand system feels underused because the cyan accent and more distinctive TikTok visual energy are largely absent. quality 78, brandAdherence 72 → score 76 (0.6·q + 0.4·b).
